channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: U.S. Officials Visited Havana to Lay Out Proposals for Cuban Reforms
item.description: The delegation told Cuba’s leadership that it had only a narrow window of time to make the economic and political changes demanded by the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:description: A street in Havana last month. During the talks last week, a State Department official said, the American delegation insisted that President Trump was open to reaching a diplomatic solution to the crisis.

item.title: Carney’s Liberal Majority Reshapes Fortunes to the Left and the Right
item.description: Mark Carney’s brand of centrist politics and his invitation to floor crossers with conservative convictions into the Liberal Party’s ranks have reshaped the country’s political economy.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Adrian Wyld/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney with new members of the Liberal caucus, from left: Tatiana Auguste (Terrebonne), Danielle Martin (University-Rosedale) and Doly Begum (Scarborough Southwest) on Wednesday in Ottawa.

item.title: Maduro’s Successor Is Purging Allies Who Kept Him in Power in Venezuela
item.description: The successor to Venezuela’s captured President Nicolás Maduro is purging the people who kept him in power.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev and Mariana Martínez
item.media:description: Delcy Rodríguez, Venezuela’s former vice president, in January in Caracas, the capital.

item.title: An Explosion Rattles a Toronto Neighborhood. A Drake Video Was to Blame.
item.description: A blast on Thursday night turned out to be for a video shoot, but it unnerved residents who had lived through a propane plant explosion in 2008.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: Drake performing in London last year. He posted two images on social media of an explosion that startled residents near Downsview Park in Toronto.

item.title: Carney Courts Investors to Reduce Canada’s Economic Dependence on U.S.
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney has set up a meeting and a new office to speed project approvals and make Canada more attractive to investors.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada on Parliament Hill this week in Ottawa.

item.title: Lutnick Says Canada Trade Deal Needs to Be Reworked Ahead of Talks
item.description: Howard Lutnick, President Trump’s commerce secretary, derided Canada’s trade strategy and said a North American deal needed to be reworked.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Yuri Gripas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Howard Lutnick, the U.S. Commerce Secretary, in Washington in February.

item.title: New York New Jersey Stadium? Why MetLife Is Changing Its Name for the World Cup
item.description: This summer, FIFA is putting its stamp on the venue for the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:description: People in New York and New Jersey know it as MetLife Stadium, but that will not be the case for the coming World Cup.

item.title: Trump Tries to Stop Drug Smuggling From South America. Drug Smugglers Invent New Tactics.
item.description: Authorities are using drones, troops and A.I. to crack down on cocaine smuggling. But gangs are finding new ways to move record amounts of drugs across the world.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova, Max Bearak and Federico Rios
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )

item.title: The Costa Rican Mountain Town Offering Sanctuary to Families Deported by Trump
item.description: In a cloud forest village, a network of residents, foreigners and pacifist Quakers offered a precarious yet vital sanctuary for families expelled by the U.S. government.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega and César Rodríguez
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Cesar Rodriguez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vusala Yusifova and her daughter, Inji, asylum seekers from Azerbaijan, cut a client’s hair in Monteverde, Costa Rica.

item.title: 3 Killed in Boat Strike in the Pacific, Pentagon Says
item.description: It was the third such attack by the U.S. military in three days, and the 51st attack in a campaign against people who the United States has accused of smuggling drugs.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Francis L. Donovan at a confirmation hearing in January to become the leader of U.S. Southern Command.

item.title: Top U.S. Diplomat in Venezuela Leaves Post After Arriving in January
item.description: Laura Dogu, who reopened the U.S. Embassy in Venezuela, returns to advising Gen. Dan Caine, the highest ranking U.S. military officer, as the war with Iran continues.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:description: Laura Dogu, the top U.S. diplomat in Venezuela, last month in the capital, Caracas.

item.title: After Fighting for the Rights of Workers, Brazil’s President Lula Faces a Labor Abuse Crisis
item.description: The dismissal of Brazil’s top labor inspector has landed the leftist government of President Lula, a former union leader, in an awkward spot.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Victor Moriyama for The New York Times
item.media:description: A factory for the automaker BYD under construction last year in Camaçari, Bahia, Brazil. A labor ministry inspection found that Chinese workers building the plant were living in squalid conditions.

item.title: Claudia Sheinbaum’s War on Crime in Mexico Faces a Grim Reckoning: 133,000 Missing People
item.description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico is under pressure to end one of her country’s most painful tragedies: the disappearance of more than 133,000 people.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega and Paulina Villegas
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Izaguirre ranch in Teuchitlán, Jalisco, where volunteer groups found charred human remains. The Jalisco New Generation Cartel had been using it as a training camp for its new recruits.

item.title: When El Salvador’s Prisoners Are Set Free, She’s There To Pick Them Up
item.description: Sugey Amaya’s brother was detained in El Salvador’s mass arrest campaign four years ago. She has devoted her life to helping prisoners like him.
item.dc:creator: Fred Ramos and Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sugey Amaya has spent the past three years waiting for her brother, Alexis, to be released from prison. He was detained under President Nayib Bukele’s state of emergency and accused of being part of a gang.

item.title: How a Times Reporter Has Covered a Turbulent Period in Latin America
item.description: Frances Robles, an international correspondent for The New York Times, has reported on the region for more than 25 years.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.category: Coups D'Etat and Attempted Coups D'Etat
item.media:description: The reporter Frances Robles, right, with the Rev. Thomas Ngigi at St. Theresa’s parish, a waterfront church in Black River, Jamaica, that was badly damaged by Hurricane Melissa last year.

item.title: Mario Ríos Montt, Bishop and Brother of Genocidal General, Dies at 94
item.description: In Guatemala, he led the Catholic Church’s human rights office. His brother, a dictator who took power in a coup, was convicted of crimes against humanity.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Bishop Mario Ríos Montt in 1999 at a news conference on human-rights atrocities committed by the military in Guatemala. “What interests me is the truth, and come what may, the truth is the truth,” he said.

item.title: Mark Carney Seals a Majority Government and Remakes Canada’s Liberal Party
item.description: The rising star in global centrist politics has secured a majority in the Canadian Parliament. Critics are crying foul.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada speaking on Saturday at a Liberal Party convention in Montreal.

item.title: Canada Special Elections 2026 Results: Carney’s Liberal Party Gains Majority
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney, constrained by leading a minority government for the past year, gained a majority in the House of Commons after special elections on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada at the Liberal Party’s convention in Montreal on Saturday. His agenda is focused on reducing Canada’s dependence on the United States.

item.title: After Chaotic Election Day, Peru Orders 2nd Day of Voting for Some Areas
item.description: Frustrated voters in long lines were unable to cast ballots in parts of the capital, Lima, due to logistical failures, eroding trust for some in the process.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky and Mitra Taj
item.media:credit: Sara Wayra for The New York Times
item.media:description: A presidential campaign event for Rafael López Aliaga, leader of the Popular Renewal Party, in Lima last Thursday.

item.title: ‘Titaníque’ on Broadway: Wild ‘Titanic’ Parody, With Celine Dion as Kooky Guide
item.description: This “Titanic” parody fueled by Dion’s hits, silly ad-libs and pop culture references had the humblest of beginnings. Now the show has docked on Broadway.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Christine Fréchette Is Chosen to Lead Coalition Avenir Québec
item.description: Far behind in the polls, Quebec’s governing party chose Christine Fréchette to succeed François Legault and to lead it in a vote this fall.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi
item.media:description: Christine Fréchette, in Drummondville, Quebec, on Sunday.

item.title: Cuba’s President Projects Defiance in Standoff With the U.S.
item.description: Miguel Díaz-Canel’s stance on “Meet the Press” reflected how Cuba is digging in against pressure from a Trump administration distracted by the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Adam B. Ellick and Simon Romero
item.media:description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel of Cuba in Havana last month. He told “Meet the Press” on Sunday, “We’ll defend ourselves, and if we need to die, we’ll die.”

item.title: Peru Votes for President, With 35 Names on the Ballot
item.description: Few expect the vote to resolve the political instability that has plagued the country for years. Three presidents have come and gone since 2021.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Sara Wayra for The New York Times
item.media:description: A campaign event for Rafael López Aliaga, leader of the Popular Renewal Party, in Lima, Peru, on Thursday.
